subheading: Run the open-source Wan video model in ComfyUI: text-to-video, image-to-video, and motion control, on Comfy Cloud or your own GPU.
extendedDescription:
If you are comparing Wan ComfyUI workflows, the first thing worth knowing is that Wan is genuinely open. It is Alibaba Tongyi Lab's open-source video family, released under Apache 2.0, which is why you can run it locally on your own hardware instead of renting it by the second. The workflows on this page are the ready-made graphs for doing exactly that: load a Wan model, give it a prompt or an image, and generate a short clip.

Wan covers the jobs people actually reach for. Text-to-video turns a written prompt into motion, image-to-video animates a still you provide, and the newer S2V variants drive a portrait from an audio clip with lip-sync. Across the family you get high temporal consistency, bilingual prompts, and multiple aspect ratios at 24 frames per second, with clips that typically land around 81 frames, roughly three and a half seconds.

Versions matter when you pick a workflow. Wan 2.1 ships in a light 1.3B size that runs on about 8GB of VRAM and a 14B size that wants 24GB or more, both at 480p and 720p, plus Fun variants for camera control and depth, pose, or canny conditioning. Wan 2.2 moves to a mixture-of-experts design for better quality at the same compute, adds a hybrid TI2V-5B model that runs on a single 4090, and includes Animate for character animation and subject replacement. Higher steps and a moderate guidance scale give cleaner motion, and the workflows expose those settings so you are not guessing.

Everything here is open and editable. Run a Wan workflow on Comfy Cloud with nothing to install when you do not have the GPU for it, or download the graph and run it locally for full control. Either way you can swap models, tune the parameters, and re-run until the clip is right, with no watermark on the result.

modelSpec.summary: Wan is Alibaba Tongyi Lab's open-source (Apache 2.0) video generation family, covering text-to-video, image-to-video, and speech-to-video across the 2.1 and 2.2 releases.
highlights: Open source under Apache 2.0, so you can run it locally or on Comfy Cloud
Text-to-video, image-to-video, and speech-to-video with lip-sync (S2V)
Wan 2.1: 1.3B size on ~8GB VRAM, 14B on 24GB+, at 480p and 720p
Wan 2.2: mixture-of-experts for higher quality, plus a TI2V-5B that runs on a single 4090
Fun variants add camera control and depth, pose, or canny conditioning
High temporal consistency, bilingual prompts, multiple aspect ratios at 24fps

howToUse: Pick a Wan workflow that matches your goal: text-to-video, image-to-video, or speech-to-video.
Open it on Comfy Cloud with no install, or load it in your local ComfyUI.
Choose the model size that fits your VRAM, for example Wan 2.1 1.3B on a smaller GPU.
Add your prompt, or upload the still image or audio the workflow expects.
Set steps and guidance scale, then run and review the motion.
Re-run with adjusted settings or a larger model, then download the clip.

whyComfy.title: Generation, animation, and editing in one family
whyComfy.description: Text-to-video, image-to-video, speech-to-video with lip-sync, and VACE editing all run from the same Wan graphs.

whyComfy.title: Pick your variant and settings
whyComfy.description: Switch between Wan 2.1 1.3B, the 14B sizes, and the Wan 2.2 TI2V-5B hybrid, then tune frames, steps, and guidance scale.

whyComfy.title: Open weights under Apache 2.0
whyComfy.description: Wan is released under Apache 2.0, so you can run it locally on your own GPU or on Comfy Cloud and use the output commercially.

whyComfy.title: No watermark on your clips
whyComfy.description: Wan generates at 24 frames per second across multiple aspect ratios, and the clips you export ship with no watermark.

faqItems.question: What are Wan ComfyUI workflows?
faqItems.answer: They are ready-made ComfyUI graphs that run Wan, the open-source video model from Alibaba's Tongyi Lab. Each loads a Wan model and wires up the inputs for a task like wan text to video or wan image to video, so you give a prompt or image and get a clip without building the graph yourself.

faqItems.question: Is Wan open source?
faqItems.answer: Yes. Wan is licensed under Apache 2.0, so the model weights are open. The ComfyUI workflows here run it locally, or on Comfy Cloud with no setup and no watermark.

faqItems.question: What can Wan generate?
faqItems.answer: Wan handles text-to-video, image-to-video, and speech-to-video with lip-sync. Across the family it produces clips at 24 frames per second with strong temporal consistency, typically around three and a half seconds long.

faqItems.question: How much VRAM does Wan need?
faqItems.answer: It depends on the size you choose. Wan 2.1 offers a 1.3B model that runs on about 8GB of VRAM, while the 14B model wants 24GB or more. Wan 2.2 adds a TI2V-5B hybrid that runs on a single 4090, and Comfy Cloud removes the VRAM requirement entirely.

faqItems.question: What is the difference between Wan 2.1 and Wan 2.2?
faqItems.answer: Wan 2.1 provides 1.3B and 14B sizes at 480p and 720p plus Fun variants for camera and depth or pose control. Wan 2.2 moves to a mixture-of-experts design for higher quality at the same compute, adds the TI2V-5B hybrid, and includes Animate for character animation and subject replacement.

faqItems.question: Can I run Wan workflows without a powerful GPU?
faqItems.answer: Yes. Run any Wan workflow on Comfy Cloud in the browser with nothing to install when your GPU is not enough. If you do have a capable card, download the workflow and run the smaller Wan 2.1 1.3B model locally.

faqItems.question: Do I need to install anything to run Wan?
faqItems.answer: Not on Comfy Cloud. The workflow opens in your browser with no install and no local setup, and the weights are already hosted, so you just add a prompt or image and run. If you prefer a local install, download the graph into your own ComfyUI and the same workflow runs on your machine.

faqItems.question: Does Wan run locally or only on Comfy Cloud?
faqItems.answer: Both. Because Wan ships open weights under Apache 2.0, you can download the model and run it on your own GPU, with the 1.3B size starting at about 8GB of VRAM. Comfy Cloud is the paid option in the browser when you would rather not manage hardware or large model files.
